name: Money & Finance
description: Coins, currency, central banks, and the basics of how the financial world works.

questions.q: Which ancient kingdom is credited with minting the world's first stamped coins around 650 BCE?
What naturally-occurring alloy of gold and silver were the first Lydian coins made from?
From which Latin word, related to a Roman temple of Juno, do we get the English word 'money'?

source.notes: The Roman mint was located near the Temple of Juno Moneta.

questions.q: Which Chinese dynasty issued the world's first paper currency in the 11th century?

source.notes: Northern Song-era jiaozi (交子) were the first widely used paper money.

questions.q: Which European bank issued the first banknotes in Europe in 1661?
choices: Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena

source.notes: Sweden's heavy copper plate currency was unwieldy, so banknotes were a welcome alternative.

questions.q: Which 1944 international conference established the post-WWII monetary order, including the IMF and World Bank?

source.notes: Held in Bretton Woods, New Hampshire.

questions.q: What ended in 1971 when President Nixon closed the gold window?
choices: Direct convertibility of U.S. dollars to gold
Use of paper money in the U.S.

source.notes: Effectively ended the Bretton Woods system.

questions.q: Money serves multiple functions. Which is NOT one of the classic four?

source.original_a: Money's a matter of functions four, A Medium, a Measure, a Standard, a Store
source.notes: Per Jevons: Medium of exchange, Unit of account, Store of value, Standard of deferred payment.

questions.q: What term describes money that has value because the government decrees it, without intrinsic worth?

source.notes: Most modern currencies are fiat. From Latin 'fiat' = let it be done.

questions.q: What is the United States' central bank called?

source.notes: Commonly called 'the Fed.' Established by the Federal Reserve Act of 1913.

questions.q: What is the U.S. central bank's main monetary-policy tool for influencing the economy?

source.notes: Set by the FOMC (Federal Open Market Committee).

questions.q: What term describes a sustained rise in the general price level of goods and services?

source.notes: When prices fall persistently it's called deflation.

questions.q: Which 1920s-era European country's currency famously experienced hyperinflation, with banknotes printed in trillions?

source.notes: Wheelbarrow-of-cash imagery dates from this period (1921–1923).

questions.q: Which African country's currency hit hyperinflation in 2008, issuing a 100 trillion-dollar note?

source.notes: Z$100,000,000,000,000 — at peak, prices doubled every 24 hours.

questions.q: Who created Bitcoin, the first decentralized cryptocurrency, in 2009?

source.notes: A pseudonym; the real identity remains publicly unknown.

questions.q: What does the acronym 'NYSE' stand for?

source.notes: Located on Wall Street; the world's largest stock exchange by market cap.

questions.q: What does GDP stand for?

source.notes: Total value of goods/services produced in a country.

questions.q: Which currency is the official money of the European Union (used by 20+ member states)?
What kind of shell was used as currency for centuries across Africa, Asia, and Oceania?

source.notes: One of the most widely-used pre-coinage 'monies' globally.

questions.q: Which Italian explorer described Chinese paper money in his 13th-century travelogue?

source.notes: Polo described mulberry-bark paper money during his time in the Mongol Yuan dynasty.
